envelopes.hydrated_text:
Methane emissions from tropical wetlands have increased by an estimated 18% over 2010-2024 according to satellite-constrained inversions, but the underlying mechanism — whether higher temperatures, increased rainfall, or expanded wetland area — has been contested. We use a process-based wetland model forced with high-resolution reanalysis and find that roughly two-thirds of the increase is attributable to temperature-driven changes in microbial activity within existing wetland area, rather than expansion of wetland area itself.

This matters for projection: temperature-driven sensitivity is well-constrained by the laboratory literature on methanogen Q10 values, while wetland-area sensitivity is poorly constrained because it depends on local hydrology that climate models do not resolve. If our attribution is correct, the methane-climate feedback in tropical wetlands is closer to the upper end of the IPCC range, with implications for cumulative warming budgets.

Uncertainties remain large enough that we do not call for revising IPCC ranges yet, but we recommend a coordinated observational campaign in the Congo basin (currently the most data-poor major tropical wetland) before AR7 is finalised.

This special report assesses the state of knowledge on methane-climate feedbacks across five reservoirs: tropical wetlands, boreal wetlands, permafrost, marine clathrates, and biomass burning. Confidence in feedback signs is high (all positive); confidence in feedback magnitudes is medium-low for all five.

The largest near-term feedback (out to 2050) is from tropical wetlands, where temperature sensitivity of methanogenesis is well-established but landscape-scale integration remains poorly constrained. Permafrost is the largest long-term concern (out to 2100 and beyond), with potentially abrupt releases possible if degradation crosses thermokarst-formation thresholds.

Marine clathrates remain low-confidence as a near-term concern: published evidence suggests release rates are slow enough relative to oxidation rates that little of the released methane reaches the atmosphere on policy-relevant timescales. We do not, however, recommend dismissing the risk entirely — geographic monitoring is sparse and the catastrophic-release scenario remains physically possible at higher warming levels.

We present a new pan-Arctic permafrost monitoring synthesis combining 412 borehole records, 28 eddy-covariance flux tower years, and high-resolution InSAR ground-deformation data. Active-layer thickness has increased by an average 27 cm since 2000 across the monitored network, with thaw rates accelerating in the last five years.

Flux measurements show that integrated annual methane emissions have not yet increased commensurate with thaw, but show a strong shift in seasonality: shoulder-season (autumn) emissions are now 2.4x their 2010 level and represent the fastest-growing component of the annual budget. Cold-season emissions, historically assumed negligible, are now 8-15% of annual totals at the better-instrumented sites.

A key open question is whether the seasonality shift will plateau or whether it presages an increase in total annual emissions over the next decade. We argue this can be answered by sustaining current monitoring through 2030, but warn that several flux towers are at risk of decommissioning due to funding gaps.

bullets: Tropical wetland methane increases over 2010-2024 are roughly two-thirds attributable to temperature-driven microbial activity rather than wetland-area expansion, which would push the methane-climate feedback toward the upper end of IPCC ranges if confirmed. (env-06-a)

Tropical wetlands carry the largest near-term (to-2050) methane feedback risk while permafrost dominates long-term (to-2100) concern, with marine clathrates remaining lower-confidence on policy-relevant timescales. (env-06-b)

Pan-Arctic permafrost data show a 27cm average active-layer thickening since 2000 and a marked shift in methane emission seasonality, with shoulder-season emissions now 2.4x their 2010 level. (env-06-c)
